author: @mangothemike
text:
Another issue: The mishandled budgeting. 

Why do movies cost over $200+ million? What movie warrants that price? And for what? VFX? Big name actors? Rewrites or reshoots? What happened to allocating that money to quality writing and marketing? If studios did that, the budgets wouldn't be so bloated and studios wouldn't have to pull hairs just to break even. 

Superman (and several others as well) both cost over 200 million, yet aren't getting anywhere close to the results they'd need to break even. 

Also, the superhero genre in general is just losing at this point. 2024 wasn't the best year in terms of quality (except Deadpool & Wolverine, an amazing movie that did make a profit), but this year took another step back. 

You know it's bad when Jurassic World, Lilo and Stitch, and Minecraft - the three worst films of this year - all made their money back.

replies.text: M. Night Shyamalan, Sam Raimi, Zack Snyder, Joss Whedon, and JJ Abrams were all hyped as the proverbial "next big thing" just because of one or two very successful contributions.  Modern day auteur theory is only going to get worse with the new Writers' Guild rules.  Tyler Sheridan's career with Paramount represents a loophole in what would otherwise be very expensive and burdensome bureaucracy - if one person writes and produces everything, you don't need to meet any kind of quotas and contracts are much simpler to follow.  Expect even more hype surrounding single creators in the future for this reason.

author: @bigmack70
text: The problem with the DCU attempts is that they just refuse to start small and build. They need focused, tightly written character pieces to tell compelling stories for individual characters, and they need to be budgeted reasonably - probably in the $100-150M range. The DCU keeps scoping their movies as if the general audience conditions are about the same as they were in Marvel Phases 1-2, and we just aren't there anymore. They keep rushing their world building and expecting that $200M+ budgeted spectacle movies are going to make money, and general audiences just aren't there anymore for that kind of thing. I actually quite enjoyed James Gunn's Superman, but I think it is better as a standalone or one-off movie than an introduction to an entirely new cinematic universe.

replies.author: @RichardPoffIII
replies.text:
As someone who is a fan of DC animation projects, trust me if DC can get a live action, cinematic universe to work, it could have real potential to be amazing.
There is a good reason why shared universes work so well in the comics and in well written movies. It’s interesting to see characters with different solo adventures and struggles and genres coming together in crossovers against threats they can’t face along and forming friendships with each other and testing each other along the way. Giving up on a shared universe closed the door on so much potential.
The real problem, in my opinion, is Warner Bros executives wanting to get to the big team up films as quickly as possible to make big money and not letting things build up naturally like in the MCU. The Snyderverse had no solo films for two thirds of the Justice league before the Justice league movie, and the new DCU begins with Supergirl already on earth, the Justice Gang already a thing, metahumans already existing for a while, and apparently the batfamily will already have been fully established if the brave and the bold movie happens.
I know some of the above were somewhat to do with Snyder and Gunn’s creative visions, but I think executive meddling could very well be a factor behind the scenes, considering that this is the second dc cinematic universe in a row that rushed into team ups too quickly.

author: @Aesthetic.Ascetic
text:
The more of a director's work you see, the more you understand their weaknesses. Much like how Zach Synder over relied on dark and gritty style over substance. And Joss Whedon over relied on his characters making quips every 5 seconds. James Gunn overly relies on making his characters a morally grey quirky band of misfits. Superman as a character never played well with Gunn's character tropes. And it really shows in how he had to make the Superman movie more into the "Justice Gang" movie.
The truth is James Gunn only ever had one good movie in him, Guardians of the Galaxy. And he has been remaking that movie over and over again in every production he has been involved in since. The GotG sequels, the Suicide Squad movie, the discount Suicide Squad tv show (Creature Commandos), and now with his new Superman aka Justice Gang movie. He is a one trick pony.

replies.author: @victorcates9330
replies.text:
He can't make the entirely of the DCU his personal sandbox. I think he wants to grow. But he doesn't have a commercial freedom to abandon James Gunnisms. His stories were young men who needed to grow or be redeemed. But Superman is something else. And peacemaker either has to stay childish or has the problem of being mature. A mature peacemaker can't go on a murder spree with vigilante as a lark. If James Gunn was using Starlord, or Rocket, or Peacemaker as vehicles for himself, then it's a problem if he's 60. If your brand is cartoonish violence and taboo, then maybe getting older means that you don't feel it anymore. But wanting to be something different doesn't mean that he has the skills for it. He's not morally sophisticated. 

Since Peacemaker s1, everything he's done has had elements of social commentary. Superman almost worked. But it was also a vehicle for Gunn's morality. Superman's parents took a dark turn, setting up his earthly parents as primary - if making them rural hicks. The highlighting of diversity was well meaning but verged on being clumsy. But then Gunn worked in an execution. Then the discussion over kindness was discussed. They spelled out their moral ideas.

Creature Commandoes had scenes of beating up misogynists (who wandered into the plot just to be monsters) and mass executing white supremacists. I think there were evil bands of incels, if memory serves.

Peacemaker season 2 complicated the morality of the alternative universe version of his father (though this was rushed). But then included an alternative universe in which white supremacists townsfolk immediately form a mob and chase a minority character SO GUNN CAN HAVE A MASS EXECUTION OF THEM. Then he has two characters flat out state that the alternative universe in which there are murals of a german chancellor isn't so different from the current US. So it did something interesting and nuanced and then did something incredibly clumsy. He also worked in some material that was religious. Which I love. But instead, we're stuck with the Peacemaker and Harcourt reltionahip. (Which sucks).

Starlord was stunted. Rocket was damaged. Peacemaker was childish. If gun wants to move beyond stories about damaged young men, then he has to solve what he needs to do next. What's the appeal of a peacemaker who doesn't kill people for the hell of it? What would you do with a Starlord who can't defend being a rogue and who has settled down? And can he change if his fans want violent pleasures? Or is he doomed to retreat his earlier work while he no longer feels it?

Of all the comments I've been reading that are just plain irrational (inclusing Critical Drinker's video here), this one makes MOST SENSE.

I DO AGREE that I'd like to have DC (even Marvel) universe that doesn't adhere to any shared universe at all.

Fortunately we have Matt Reeve's Batman as a prime example of that, and it performed great when it came out.

However, I can also see the necessity to STILL HAVE a shared universe in these Marvel and DC movie projects. Because as fans, who wouldn't love to see your favorite heroes showing up on other movies starring also other favorite heroes from time to time, sharing the same external struggles while each speaking for their own philosophies and fighting their own internal battles?

author: @christopherthesecond
text:
1. James Gunn is at his core, a director more than writer. Director's elevate scripts and polish them, not write, and produce without a team of talented creatives to act as barometers. Giving a man who's never had decades long plans authority over a franchise instead of atleast testing him with co-written projects was bad for business.
2. The real reason for superhero flops is bad storytelling. The 4 core elements of plot, emotional experience, memorability and rewatchability are either missing or poorly handled, leaving general audiences and fans disappointed - nobody wants bad movies after paying to watch them again and again.
3. The dcu can thrive if they find a visionary who loves the franchise too. Start over with a slate of movies that progress naturally towards bigger events. Marvel despite being poorly handled lately is past some major comic events and we haven't even had any crisis except a bad flashpoint from DC. Gunn's mix of important characters with movies of obscure characters with no emotional and financial weight isn't helping. The only promising thing was a green lantern detective series which sounded bound to build up to a decent justice league movie.

replies.author: @jrd33
replies.text: Patty Jenkins did a fine job of directing Wonder Woman, but a lousy job of writing Wonder Woman 1984. But I guess big egos and massive overconfidence are par for the course for a successful Hollywood director.

replies.author: @TooFatTooFurious
replies.text:
1. This is a huge mistunderstanding of what directors do. There's lots of different directing styles. Some directors are just guns for hire who mostly just shoot the movie and that's about it, but plenty are absolutely super involved as writers, producers and directors. Also, Gunn had a decades long plans authority over a franchise before: it was called "Guardians of the Galaxy" and it was super successful. 
2. Great news: Gunn handles all core elements of storytelling pretty well, as evidenced by the fact that both critics and audiences clearly liked "Superman" and the two TV shows he wrote. 
3. James Gunn is exactly the kind of visionary you're describing here, and he's doing what you're claiming he should be doing. He did start small with two tv projects before moving on to Superman. And why is mixing important characters with obscure ones is bad? When MCU started out, characters like Thor, Captain America, Black Panther and Captain Marvel were virtually unkown to an average moviegoer. Yet they all became huge hits.

replies.author: @micotellsart
replies.text: @TooFatTooFuriousI respectfully argue against point 2. At least in Superman, there were two main story elements he did really bad: structure and selling a conflict. The structure was extremely messy, the rising action was inconsistent, if felt like it didnt had a proper begging but if we were missing something, and its not really due to the in-media-res. And with “not selling the conflict” I mean that really didnt sold well the conflict between the two nations in the beggining, they just TELL you about it but dont really show it and presented it well using actions. And the plot thread of people hating superman bia internet (including the thing with the monkeys) also didnt really impacted the plot. If in a crucial moment, people saw how Superman was good and they used collective effort to help in a battle, them it would have felt relevant, specially if they used social media to showcase How Superman was good and that inspired people to fight. Thats why the clímax feels kinda anti-climatic, because many plotlines felt either abandoned or given a resolution by the wrong characters (like Green Lantern saving the little boy in the military conflict, instead of Superman). I generally liked the movie, but Thats why I think its a 6/10. Enjoyable, but with a lot of wasted potential and with an overcomplicated climax in which the plot about the war and the plot of the rift in Metropolis feel disconected, when the movie would have been better if they just choose one, like the plot of the war, and How Superman fighted in it and inspired the other heroes to stop being corporate-bootlickers and help people just for the desire of helping. But that couldnt happen bc Gunn didnt even made Maxwell Lord a real villain even though making that decission and making him collaborate with Lex, would have improved the movie in general by giving it a more concrete Message

author: @shurik121
text:
The problem with James Gunn headlining a major franchise is that he's at his best when working on some wacky concepts, in a punk-rock kind of way. You can take James Gunn out of Troma, but you can't take Troma out of James Gunn. When he has to limit himself to PG13 kind of movies - at best we get GotG movies but looks like he had to limit himself too much for Superman (it was OK, but I wanted it to be more of a James Gunn kind of movie). When he has no limitations and an R rating - we get Slither, Super or The Suicide Squad.

I don't have any issues with him casting his family or friends - almost all of his movies since the Troma days had his brother in some role, and he has other regulars in almost everything (even cameos by Lloyd Kaufman). Jenna Fischer was in Slither when they were married. That's not his problem. His problem, IMO, is that he should continue making James Gunn movies instead of trying to make a generic Marvel-type slop.

Can I just point this out for the comment section? No one is saying you can't like Superman or Peacemaker or that other show. Do I think this movie and those two shows will get the normies back on board with superhero cinematic universes? 
Not exactly. It certainly doesn't look that way, so far. We needed a great Superman movie, we need a great Batman movie, and probably a movie combining the two, because those characters are the faces of DC. Niche projects work for geeks, but they can't sustain those movie budgets on geek money alone.

It is perfectly fine to like flawed movies (and I'd certainly hope so, because every movie is flawed). Probably shouldn't over exaggerate the quality of a movie you really like, if it's not a great movie. I love Van Hellsing and the first Milla Jovovich Resident Evil movie, but they're not great movies, objectively.

author: @Cybroto
text: Drinker, you are wrong again here man. The movie was a success. Warner Bros was less worried about the money and more worried about public reception. It is the first DC movie to be widely regarded as a good movie by both fans and critics in a long time. The movie also did extremely well in the box office domestically. It did not do so well abroad...and we can talk about that (I think it is because the rest of the world hates America right now). In general the movie did exactly what it needed to do. It got fans back in the box office for a DC movie, which is crazy given the last few movies that came out from DC. It also still holds a strong 83% from critics and 90% from audiences which is amazing for this movie, and probably overperformed WB's expectations. I honestly think you are just being negative to be negative and not being objective about this. Also, peacemaker is pretty good, it is not great but it's entertaining. Creature commandos was also really good. I have been let down by superhero stuff for a long time now and Superman made me excited about what is to come from DC. So I really disagree with you on this one.
